target: WIN-038 argument rewrite — lead with pole-floor bound from Section 2.1. Replaces detail_evidence (primary), updates finding, tldr_evidence, and tldr_verdict to make the pole-floor impossibility the lead argument rather than the weaker averaging framing. Averaging backup kept as reinforcement.

patches.replacement_text: Dome's own cavity minimum is 8.78 Hz at the pole (its most favorable point); 7.83 Hz is below the geometric floor at every radius; stability is textbook ionospheric physics

The dome's own height formula H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km has its maximum at r = 0, giving a minimum quarter-wave frequency of 8.78 Hz at the pole and rising monotonically outward (27 Hz at r = 10,000 km, ~90 Hz at the rim). 7.83 Hz sits below that floor at every single point, so no averaging scheme can rescue it. Standard ionospheric physics already explains 7.83 Hz via Earth's 40,075 km circumference with D-layer damping.

The dome's own cavity cannot produce 7.83 Hz at any radius — 8.78 Hz is its pole-floor minimum, and the frequency only rises from there. Standard globe ionospheric physics explains the observed frequency and its stability.

patches.replacement_html:
The pole-floor impossibility (lead argument). The dome's own cavity height formula H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km has a strict global maximum of 8,537 km at r = 0 and decreases monotonically outward. Applying the dome's own quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H) — the same formula WIN-029 uses to back-derive a 9,572 km ceiling from 7.83 Hz — to this height profile gives the dome's minimum possible resonance frequency at the pole: f(r = 0) = 299,792 / (4 × 8,537) ≈ 8.78 Hz. Because H(r) falls monotonically, f(r) rises monotonically: at r = 5,960 km (the dome's published Chapel Hill latitude) H ≈ 4,278 km and f ≈ 17.5 Hz; at r = 10,000 km, f ≈ 27 Hz; at the 20,015 km disc rim, f ≈ 90 Hz. 7.83 Hz is below the dome's own geometric floor at every point on the disc. No weighting scheme — area-weighted, radial, energy-weighted, or infinitely pole-concentrated — can drive an average below 8.78 Hz when 8.78 Hz is the minimum of the function being averaged. This is not a calibration question; it is a mathematical impossibility inherited directly from the dome's published H(r).

Averaging backup (reinforcement, not the main argument). Even setting the pole-floor aside, no physically motivated spatial average over the disc lands anywhere near 7.83 Hz either. Effective heights ranging from ~1,800 km (area-weighted) to ~5,200 km (energy-weighted) yield predicted fundamentals of 14–42 Hz, a spread that brackets the simple radial average (~3,400 km → ~22 Hz). Every averaging choice is wrong, and all of them are wrong in the same direction: higher than observed. The averaging framing is a softer way to state what the pole-floor already settles absolutely.

Dome's own triple-count. WIN-002 (damping explains 10.59 → 7.83), WIN-029 (7.83 back-derives H = 9,572 km), and WIN-038 (7.83 remains stable) all derive from a single Tomsk Schumann measurement. WIN-029's back-derived ceiling of 9,572 km exceeds the dome's own V12 maximum of 8,537 km at r = 0 by ~12% — a second-order internal contradiction (the dome's observed-frequency-to-cavity-height inversion demands a cavity taller than its own formula permits). See Section 2.1 for the full pole-floor and supremum-impossibility treatment, and WIN-029 / WIN-002 for the duplicate-counting cross-reference.

Stability is ionospheric physics, not dome evidence. The long-term stability of the Schumann fundamental near 7.83 Hz is well-established in standard atmospheric physics (Sentman 1987; Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002, ISBN 978-1-4020-0754-5). The frequency varies diurnally by ~0.5 Hz and shifts by up to ~1 Hz during major geomagnetic storms, but the decadal mean sits within ±0.1 Hz. A model whose own geometry forbids the observed frequency cannot claim credit for that frequency remaining stable; stability of a value you cannot produce is not a confirmation, and both the globe and the dome would predict stability of any resonance their cavity supports — stability is not a discriminating test.

holes_addressed: ISS-1270 / WIN-038.c3 H3 (moderate): 'WIN-level argument is STRICTLY WEAKER than Section 2.1's.' Addressed by leading with the pole-floor bound (8.78 Hz minimum at r = 0, frequency rises monotonically) and demoting the 14–42 Hz averaging result to a reinforcement paragraph.

WIN-038.c3 H4 (moderate): post-hoc wording conflated Schumann's 1952 theoretical prediction with first measurement. Although this expansion focuses on detail_evidence, the tldr_verdict is also rephrased to avoid that conflation, and the detail_verdict_text is left unchanged because it already draws the theoretical-vs-measured distinction correctly (König & Schumann 1954 is cited there).
holes_not_addressed_in_this_exp: WIN-038.c3 H5 (minor): anchor id 'part2' resolves to Part 2 top, not specifically to '2.1 Schumann Resonance'. Out of scope for an argument rewrite. Can be fixed in a separate integrity item that adds id='p2-21' to the details wrapper in sections.json part2.

WIN-038.c3 H6 (minor): detail_extra duplicates the OTS canary framing already in detail_verdict_text. Kept as-is because the detail_extra block is a one-line pointer that terminates in the 'See Timestamp Error' link — not substantive duplication.
EXP-198 (separate item): defense-neutralization against the transverse-mode best-defense. That is a standalone mode-3 expansion and is not resolved here.
new_evidence_added: Explicit quarter-wave computation at r = 0, r = 5,960, r = 10,000, and r = 20,015 km, showing monotonic rise from 8.78 Hz to ~90 Hz — traceable to raw-text/05-model.txt lines 16 and 34.

Explicit statement that WIN-029's back-derived ceiling (9,572 km) exceeds WIN-038's own H(r = 0) ceiling (8,537 km), flagging the second-order dome-internal contradiction that Section 2.1 already highlighted but WIN-038 had not.

anticipated_objections.objection: The quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H) is a small-resonator approximation, not a planetary-scale mode formula. You are attacking a straw-man model.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome itself is on record using f = c/(4H). Model page 05-model.txt line 64 explicitly performs the inversion H = c/(4 × 7.83) = 9,572 km to back out a cavity height from the observed Schumann fundamental. A model that uses the quarter-wave relation to derive its own numbers cannot disown it when the forward direction contradicts the observed frequency. If the dome wishes to retreat to a transverse mode instead, see EXP-198; the short version is that the disc-diameter-matches-globe-circumference coincidence damning such a retreat is a Recurring Pattern #4 (copy-globe-geometry) fingerprint, not a dome derivation.

anticipated_objections.objection: Apply an infinitely pole-weighted average (or just 'use H at the pole') and you get 8.78 Hz, which is only 12% off 7.83 Hz — close enough.
anticipated_objections.response: 12% is not close enough on a model that is supposed to predict the frequency, and this is the most-favorable case. The dome's geometry cannot produce 7.83 Hz at any radius; 8.78 Hz is its absolute minimum. Calibrating post-hoc to the pole point still leaves a residual, and the residual cannot be closed without either increasing H above the dome's own V12 8,537 km ceiling (directly contradicting the published formula) or switching to a different mode structure (see previous objection).

anticipated_objections.objection: Maybe H(r) applies to something other than the Schumann cavity — the 7.83 Hz resonance could live in a different sub-layer of the firmament.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's own model page uses H(r) and f = c/(4H) to derive the Schumann cavity height (9,572 km on line 64), then asserts the firmament 'near the pole' is that height. If a different sub-layer is now supplying the Schumann cavity, the dome owes us the formula for that sub-layer, a forward derivation to 7.83 Hz from dome physics, and an explanation of why the stated V12 H(r) is not the applicable object. Without that, 'a different sub-layer' is an unspecified-escape-hatch defense, exactly the kind of post-hoc adjustment the Section 3.1b standard rejects.

kernel_of_truth: A closed resonant cavity with a conductive upper boundary does produce stable electromagnetic resonances whose frequencies are determined by the cavity's geometry — that is correct physics, and it is precisely how the standard Schumann resonance works between the Earth's surface and the ionospheric D-layer. The dome's reasoning chain 'conductive shell → resonant EM cavity → Schumann resonances' is accurate in isolation, and the observation that a measured resonance constrains cavity height via a mode equation is standard. The problem is not with cavity physics in general but with this specific cavity: the dome's published H(r) geometry does not produce 7.83 Hz at any radius. Correct general reasoning does not rescue a specific geometry whose forward prediction is wrong, and the stability of a textbook-stable phenomenon across a 6-day observation window has no discriminating power between the two models — both predict stability of any resonance their cavity supports.
tldr_review_notes: The existing tldr_evidence led with 'effective average gives ~22 Hz' (a softer 2.8× miss). The new tldr_evidence leads with the pole-floor bound (an unrecoverable impossibility) and mentions averaging as the secondary frame. The existing tldr_verdict punchline was 'cavity predicts ~22 Hz' — changed to '8.78 Hz is the pole-floor minimum, 7.83 Hz is below it at every radius'. Both new TLDRs keep to 2–3 sentences and put the punchline first, per the Progressive Disclosure standard.
verdict_text_unchanged_reason: detail_verdict_text already correctly frames the post-hoc / OTS / König & Schumann 1954 narrative after its cycle-3 rewrite. The curmudgeon's H4 recommendation (distinguish 1952 theoretical prediction from 1954 first measurement) is already satisfied in the current detail_verdict_text. Rewriting it again risks regressing those corrections. The tldr_verdict is updated here because it was out-of-sync with the Section 2.1 pole-floor framing.
cross_section_propagation: None required for this item. Section 2.1 already leads with the pole-floor argument; WIN-038 is catching up to Section 2.1, not pushing new content into it. WIN-029 and WIN-002 detail_evidence should eventually carry analogous pole-floor language, but that is a separate follow-on (not triggered by this curmudgeon review) and would be its own EXP.
cross_references_added: Explicit in-text links to WIN-029 and WIN-002 in the new detail_evidence triple-count paragraph.
Section 2.1 anchor (#part2) preserved as in the prior detail_evidence; no change to the link target.
monitoring_tag_note: Code-analysis tags unchanged (monitoring=hardcoded, relabels_standard=true, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false). The rewrite strengthens the derives_from_dome=false case by making the forward-prediction impossibility the lead argument rather than a derived-from-averaging calculation.
integrity_notes: All numerical values in the replacement_html are computed from the dome's own V12 parameters (H(r = 0) = 8,537 km, c = 299,792 km/s, disc_radius = 20,015 km) using f = c/(4H). The pole-floor value 8.78 Hz rounds from 8.779… which matches Section 2.1's 8.77 Hz within rounding of c (the c used in Section 2.1 was 300,000 km/s). The Chapel Hill radius 5,960 km is the dome's own published number (raw-text/05-model.txt line 35).
